title: Velocity-field characteristics and device performance in nanoscale amorphous oxide Thin-Film-Transistors
authors: Chankeun Yoon, Xiao Wang, Jatin Vikram Singh, Sanjay K. Banerjee, Ananth Dodabalapur
abstract: The electron velocity-electric field characteristics in short channel length (50-100 nm) amorphous oxide field-effect transistors (FETs) are described using measured experimental data from indium gallium zinc oxide (IGZO) FETs in conjunction with a physics-based model. Such understanding is crucial for the design of FETs for emerging applications such as in back-end-of-line circuitry for advanced memories and artificial intelligence hardware. In such semiconductor systems, there is an interplay between trapping and extended state (band) transport that has to be considered in detail for a more complete physical understanding of device operation. The approach described in this paper demonstrates such a method and its use for an exemplary semiconductor IGZO. It can be used in many emerging thin-film semiconductors, including several amorphous oxide semiconductors. The carrier mobility is calculated for dominant scattering mechanisms such as trapped carrier scattering and optical phonon scattering. The carrier velocity is computed from the mobility using a modified Caughey-Thomas equation. The physical model considers contact resistance, Joule heating, and electric-field-induced carrier heating, all of which are very important in small geometry FETs. The carrier velocity exhibits a tendency to saturate at high electric fields and reaches values > 2*10^6 cm/s when averaged over all induced carriers (both trapped and in the band) and > 4*10^6 cm/s for carriers in the band.
summary: This paper investigates the electron transport characteristics in extremely small (50-100 nm) amorphous IGZO transistors. By using a physics-based model that accounts for trapping, scattering, and heating, the authors show how carrier velocity saturates at high electric fields. They also demonstrate that contact resistance becomes a dominant factor in device performance at these scales. The findings are crucial for designing next-generation electronics like AI hardware and advanced memory.
main_problem: The paper addresses the lack of detailed understanding regarding high-field charge transport, carrier velocity, and the impact of contact resistance and heating in nanoscale amorphous oxide transistors.
main_result: The study demonstrates that carrier velocity in IGZO FETs saturates at high electric fields, reaching values > 2*10^6 cm/s for all carriers and > 4*10^6 cm/s for band carriers, while also quantifying the dominance of contact resistance in the sub-100 nm regime.
method: The researchers used electrical measurements (DC and pulsed) on 50-100 nm IGZO FETs combined with a self-consistent quasi-2D analytical framework based on the Multiple Trap and Release (MTR) mechanism.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of nanoscale (50-100 nm) amorphous Indium Gallium Zinc Oxide (IGZO) thin-film transistors. The model incorporates the MTR mechanism, accounting for the interplay between trapped and extended state transport, scattering (trapped carrier and polar optical phonon), and thermal effects (Joule and carrier heating).
key_observables: Carrier velocity (v), effective mobility (mu_eff), electric field (E), carrier density (trapped, band, and total), and effective carrier temperature (T_eff).
important_parameters: Channel lengths of 50-100 nm, high electric fields (>= 10^4 V/cm), and the transport reduction factor (P_TRF).
main_assumptions: Hopping transport is neglected; carriers in trap states are assumed not to contribute to conduction; some band carriers are assumed not to contribute due to dynamic localization; the DOS of extended states is assumed to be 2D.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the MTR mechanism and DOS; Figure 2 shows device schematics, SEM images, and contact resistance extraction; Figure 5 shows velocity-field curves demonstrating saturation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nanoscale transport, describes the MTR-based physical model and experimental platform, presents results on contact resistance and voltage drops, details the extraction of spatial profiles for electric field and velocity, and concludes with the implications for device scaling and material quality.
why_interesting: While primarily an engineering/device physics paper, the study of transport in amorphous systems involves complex interplay between localization, trapping, and field-induced dynamics, which relates to the physics of disordered conductors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic illustration of charge transport in amorphous IGZO based on multiple trap and release (MTR) mechanism. Carriers are excited from localized trap states into extended states. Only carriers with a path length (l) larger than lattice constant (a) contribute effectively to conduction. (b) Density of states (DOS) distribution in amorphous IGZO. Localized trap states extend into the bandgap below the band edge, while extended states above the band edge contribute to charge transport.

Figure 2. (a) Schematic illustration of bottom-gate, top-contact IGZO thin-film-transistor, showing major resistance components: source contact resistance (Rs), drain contact resistance (RD) and channel resistance (Rchannel). (b) Top-view scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of 100 nm Lch device. (scale bar: 100 nm) (c) Total resistance (Rtotal) as function of channel length measured at VDS = 0.1 V for different carrier densities. Symbols represent experimental data and solid lines are linear fits. (d) Extracted Rc obtained from the y-intercepts of the linear fits to Rtotal vs. Lch in Fig. 2c. Error bars represent the standard errors of the fitted intercepts. (e) Rc/Rtotal and (f)...
Figure 2a shows the device structure of the bottom-gate, top-contact IGZO thin-film-transistor
